title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready: What the Docs Do Not Say
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The Veltrix configuration decisions around events that most operators get wrong and the structured approach that gets them right.

content.text: After deployment, operators discover that the Treasure Hunt Engine often fails to launch correctly when the events.yml and prizes.yml files are used as shipped from the Veltrix package. The core issue is the lack of default fallbacks for the cron‑style launch timings and the absence of a validity check for prize tier definitions. As a result, if an operator forgets to specify a 'start_time' for a Monday treasure round, the engine silently ignores the round and no loot spawns, breaking community expectations. Operators therefore need to dig into Veltrix documentation only after they experience silent failures on live servers, because the default configuration bundled with the engine does not expose the subtle but critical parameters that control event scheduling and prize allocation.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For
Moreover, the naive use of the placeholder 'null' for unknown values results in the engine misinterpreting the schedule, causing events to cascade back-to-back.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

The architecture of a Treasure Hunt Engine event is defined almost entirely by a handful of keys in events.yml and prizes.yml. Setting “event.type: TreasureHunt” locks the entire system into locating the correct hunt module. The “participant_limit” key dictates how many concurrent searchers a server can support; a default of 64 enables mass participation but rapidly stresses the spawning logic, so operators often lower it to 24 to keep latency low and rewards meaningful. “allow_late_entry” toggles whether players can join after the hunt begins; disabling it pushes participants to log in right at the official launch, synchronizing behavior and ensuring a steady influx of players. “prize_pool.base” and “reward_distribution” in prizes.yml shape the perceived value of loot drops; if “base” is set too low, players feel the hunt is unrewarding and drop out, whereas a well‑calculated “distribution” curve keeps the mystery alive. All of these parameters influence how players time their logins, how often they return for repeat hunts, and ultimately how engaged they remain with the server.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

Scheduling is the heartbeat that drives login habits, and in Veltrix a small misstep in events.yml can ripple across the entire player base. The “timing.start” and “timing.end” entries must be set in UTC or the server’s local offset; otherwise, a game‑changer might begin hours earlier or later than advertised, causing confusion. “repeat_interval” controls how frequently the same event recurs; setting this to “daily” is tempting, but if the “announce.delay_before” is also set to ten minutes, the announcement drifts in front of the event launch, leaving players unsynced. Operators often misconfigure “announce.frequency” to trigger every minute, creating a barrage of chat spam that pushes players to mute notifications, or worse, turn the server off entirely. A single mis‑tuned “start_time” can also cause the event to launch while the server is still booting up or while the maximum player attendance is already reached, which throws off the reward distribution and ruins the expected thrill.
If the announce.frequency is set too high, players may miss critical clues; set it too low and they lose motivation.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

1. Begin with events.yml ensuring the event name, start_time, and duration are correctly defined. 2. Populate prizes.yml with a well‑structured prize_pool list that references each reward ID used in the event. The prize_gathering_mode must match the event type. 3. Configure announce.yml, setting announce_interval to a value that balances visibility and chat hygiene, and use the correct placeholder tags. 4. For multi‑round activities, edit tournament.yml to seed the bracket and set match_duration, so each participant group receives fair playtime. 5. Finally, load the event into Veltrix, test the full cycle, verify that the Treasure Hunt Engine VX-EVT-01 spawns items at the scheduled coordinates, grants prizes, and that the announce stream is visible.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence
Count words: Operators1 should2 routinely3 audit4 these5 configurations6 before7 launch8 to9 catch10 invisible11 misalignments12 early13.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior
Post title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready: What the Docs Do Not Say. Category: Events. Write THREE items separated by blank lines.

At the heart of the Veltrix events.yml lies a handful of knobs that determine how a Treasure Hunt Engine feels in production. The most impactful parameter is eventDurationMillis, which defines the length of each hunt in milliseconds. For a 50‑player arena, a 30‑minute window (1,800,000 ms) usually suffices; as you grow to 200 players, extending to 45 minutes (2,700,000 ms) keeps seekers engaged without overwhelming them. Next comes maxParticipants. On a small server, setting it to 100 caps the queue and preserves server resources; escalating to 300 on a 500‑player world allows many players to stay in the loop. spawnRate per minute dictates how often new clues appear; 4/​min works well at 100 players but you should drop it to 3/​min for 500 players to avoid block lag. rewardPrecision, the number of decimal places for random loot values, should be 2 on low‑count servers and tightened to 0 on larger clans to streamline the distribution engine. Lastly, feedbackDebounceMs controls the cooldown on audit logs—setting 5,000 ms on a 50‑player map keeps the log tidy, while a 10,000 ms debounce on a 1000‑player run prevents flooding.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

At the heart of the Veltrix events.yml lies a handful of knobs that determine how a Treasure Hunt Engine feels in production. The most impactful parameter is eventDurationMillis, which defines the length of each hunt in milliseconds. For a 50‑player arena, a 30‑minute window (1,800,000 ms) usually suffices; as you grow to 200 players, extending to 45 minutes (2,700,000 ms) keeps seekers engaged without overwhelming them. Next comes maxParticipants. On a small server, setting it to 100 caps the queue and preserves server resources; escalating to 300 on a 500‑player world allows many players to stay in the loop. spawnRate per minute dictates how often new clues appear; 4/​min works well at 100 players but you should drop it to 3/​min for 500 players to avoid block lag. rewardPrecision, the number of decimal places for random loot values, should be 2 on low‑count servers and tightened to 0 on larger clans to streamline the distribution engine. Lastly, feedbackDebounceMs controls the cooldown on audit logs—setting 5,000 ms on a 50‑player map keeps the log tidy, while a 10,000 ms debounce on a 1000‑player run prevents flooding.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Ensure the events.yml contains a properly formatted Treasure Hunt event block with a valid event ID, start time, end time, and a list of spawn locations. Verify that prizes.yml references the event ID, lists all reward items with correct rarities, and that each prize has an associated spawn probability. Confirm that announce.yml schedules pre‑event and post‑event announcements, with clear timing offsets relative to the event start and end times, and that the announcement messages mention the Treasure Hunt theme. Check that tournament.yml is not inadvertently configured for the Treasure Hunt, or if it is, confirm that the brackets are disabled or set to a separate category. Validate that all YAML syntax is correct, with no indentation errors, and that the Veltrix server has enabled the Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 plugin before launching. Confirm that player permissions include the “treasurehunt.participant” role, and that the event’s internal cooldown period is respected to prevent spamming. Finally, run a dry‑run simulation to ensure that items spawn correctly, rewards are granted, and announcements trigger at the designated times. Early detection of any missing or misconfigured YAML directives prevents downtime during the live event, guaranteeing a smooth participant experience across the Hytale server.

Configuring Veltrix for Treasure Hunt events is all about meticulous YAML alignment—ensure every referenced file points to the same event ID, and double‑check cron schedules in announce.yml to avoid announcement gaps.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
